elements.sent-2411-001: We obtain a generic regularity result for stationary integral $n$-varifolds assumed to have only strongly isolated singularities inside $N$-dimensional Riemannian manifolds, without any restriction on the dimension ($n\geq 2$) and codimension.
elements.sent-2411-002: As a special case, we prove that for any $n\geq 2$ and any compact $(n+1)$-dimensional manifold $M$ the following holds: for a generic choice of the background metric $g$ all stationary integral $n$-varifolds in $(M,g)$ will either be entirely smooth or have at least one singular point that is not strongly isolated.
elements.sent-2411-003: In other words, for a generic metric only ``more complicated'' singularities may possibly persist.
elements.sent-2411-004: This implies, for instance, a generic finiteness result for the class of all closed minimal hypersurfaces of area at most $4\pi^2-\varepsilon$ (for any $\varepsilon>0$) in nearly round four-spheres: we can thus give precise answers, in the negative, to the questions of persistence of the Clifford football and of Hsiang's hyperspheres in nearly-round metrics.
elements.sent-2411-005: The aforementioned main regularity result is achieved as a consequence of the fine analysis of the Fredholm index of the Jacobi operator for such varifolds: we prove on the one hand an exact formula relating that number to the Morse indices of the conical links at the singular points, while on the other hand we show that such an index is non-negative for all such varifolds if the ambient metric is generic.
elements.sent-2411-006: In 1983, Hsiang \cite{Hsi83 solved Chern's spherical Bernstein conjecture by constructing a sequence of infinitely many, pairwise distinct, embedded minimal hyperspheres in the round four-dimensional sphere.}
elements.sent-2411-007: This result is to be compared with what happens in one dimension less, as determined by the earlier rigidity theorems obtained by Almgren \cite{Alm66 and Calabi \cite{Cal67 .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Such hyperspheres are constructed by means of equivariant methods and the analysis of the resulting singular ODE system in the quotient space.
elements.sent-2411-009: It turns out that their (unit multiplicity) varifold limit is the spherical suspension of a Clifford torus inside an equatorial three-sphere, thus a singular minimal subvariety (henceforth refereed to as Clifford football), which is smooth at all points except the north and south pole.
elements.sent-2411-010: It is an interesting question, brought to the attention of the first-named author about a decade ago by Neves, whether such a picture persists when considering nearly-round metrics on $S^4$.

elements.sent-2411-012: This question stems from the well-known link between rigidity phenomena characterizing special submanifolds in round spheres, and the resulting scarcity phenomena for slightly deformed metrics.

elements.section-2411-007: A three-circle inequality and related decay estimates
elements.sent-2411-540: The following growth rate monotonicity formula is a direct consequence of the decomposition (\ref{Equ_Pre_Decomp Jac into homogen Jac field ), which was in fact first introduced in \cite{Sim83 in a different form.}}
elements.sent-2411-541: For any $\sigma>0$, there exists $K = K(\sigma)>2$ with the following property.
elements.sent-2411-542: For any $\gamma\in \RR$ and any minimal cone $\mbfC\in \cC_{N,n}$ satisfying \begin{equation}\dist_\RR (\gamma, \Gamma(\mbfC)\cup \{-(n-2)/2\}) \geq \sigma\,,\end{equation} if $u\in C^2_{loc (\mbfA(K^{-3 , 1);\mbfV)\cap L^2(\mbfA(K^{-3 , 1);\mbfV)$ solves $L_\mbfC u = 0$ then its growth rate \begin{equation}J_K^\gamma (u; r):= \int_{\mbfA(K^{-1}r, r)} |u|^2\cdot |x|^{-2\gamma - n} d\|\mbfC\|\,,\end{equation} satisfies the inequality \begin{equation}J_K^\gamma(u; K^{-2}) - 2 J_K^\gamma(u; K^{-1}) + J_K^\gamma(u; 1) \geq 0\,.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-543: Moreover, equality holds if and only if $u\equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-544: Recall that, consistently with the general notational convetions we have stipulated in Section \ref{sec:Prelim we have denoted by $\mbfV=\mbfV(\mbfC)$ the normal bundle to $\mbfC$ in $\R^{N}$ and by $\mbfA(r, s):= \AAa(\mathbf{0 , r, s)\cap \mbfC$ the annular region, on the cone, of radii $r<s$.}}
elements.sent-2411-545: By \eqref{Equ_Pre_Decomp Jac into homogen Jac field -\eqref{Equ_Pre_Homogen Jac field , see Section \ref{subs:AnalysisCone1 , we have \begin{equation}J^\gamma_K(v; r) = \int_{K^{-1}r}^r t^{n-1-n-2\gamma}\ dt\int_{\mbfC\cap \SSp^{N-1}} v(t, \omega)^2\ d\omega = \sum_{j\geq 1}\underbrace{\int_{K^{-1}r}^r t^{-1-2\gamma}\cdot (v_j^+(t)+v_j^-(t))^2\ dt}_{=:\ T_j},\end{equation} where of course the second equality relies upon Parseval's identity, with respect to the orthonormal (Hilbertian) basis $\varphi_1,\varphi_2,\ldots,\varphi_k,\ldots$ of the space $L^2(\mbfC\cap\SSp^{N-1 ;\mbfV)$ of square-integrable normal sections to the link $S:=\mbfC\cap\SSp^{N-1}$ in the unit sphere of Euclidean $\R^N$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-153: \begin{equation}J^\gamma_K(v; r) = \int_{K^{-1}r}^r t^{n-1-n-2\gamma}\ dt\int_{\mbfC\cap \SSp^{N-1}} v(t, \omega)^2\ d\omega = \sum_{j\geq 1}\underbrace{\int_{K^{-1}r}^r t^{-1-2\gamma}\cdot (v_j^+(t)+v_j^-(t))^2\ dt}_{=:\ T_j},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-546: Recalling the definition of $\mu_j=\lambda_j+(n-1)$ where $\lambda_j$ is the $j$-th eigenvalue of the Jacobi operator of the link $S$ in question (see the second part of Section \ref{subs:FirstSecVar ), if $\mu_j\geq -(n-2)^2/4$ then we have \begin{equation}T_j = \int_{K^{-1}r}^r \left(c_j^+ t^{\gamma_j^+ -\gamma} + c_j^- t^{\gamma_j^- - \gamma} \right)^2 t^{-1}\ dt, \  if \mu_j > -\frac{(n-2)^2}{4}; T_j = \int_{K^{-1}r}^r \left(c_j^+ t^{-(n-2)/2 -\gamma} + c_j^- t^{-(n-2)/2 - \gamma}\log t\right)^2 t^{-1}\ dt, \  if \mu_j = -\frac{(n-2)^2}{4};\end{equation} while if $\mu_j< -(n-2)^2/4$, set $\alpha_j:= \sqrt{-\mu_j-(n-2)^2/4}$, $2c_j^+ =: c_j e^{i\theta_j}$ (with $c_j\in\R$), then \begin{equation}T_j = \int_{K^{-1}r}^r c_j^2 \left(\cos(\alpha_j\log(t)+\theta_j)t^{-(n-2)/2-\gamma}\right)^2 t^{-1}\ dt\,.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-549: Then there exists a real number $K_0 = K_0(\sigma)>2$ with the following property.
elements.sent-2411-550: For any $\alpha, \beta, \theta\in \RR$ such that $\lambda>0, |\beta| \geq \sigma$ and any $K\geq K_0$, the integral \begin{equation}\cI_K(r) := \int_r^{Kr}\cos^2(\alpha\log(s)+\theta)\, s^{2\beta-1}\ ds,\end{equation} satisfies for every $r>0$, \begin{equation}\cI_K(K^2r) - 2\cI_K(Kr) + \cI_K(r) > 0\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-551: Notice that \begin{equation}\int_r^{Kr} \cos^2(\alpha\log(s) + \theta)\, s^{2\beta-1}\ ds = \int_{\log(r)+\theta/\alpha}^{\log(r)+\theta/\alpha +\log(K)} \cos^2(\alpha\tau)\, e^{2\beta\tau-2\beta\theta/\alpha}\ d\tau\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-552: Therefore, if we let $r':=\log(r)+\theta/\alpha$, $K':=\log(K)$, $\beta':= 2\beta$, $\alpha'=2\alpha$ and \begin{equation}\tilde{\cI}_{K'}(r'):= \int_{r'}^{r'+K'} 2\cos^2(\alpha\tau)\, e^{\beta'\tau}\ d\tau = \int_{r'}^{r'+K'} \left(1+\cos(\alpha'\tau)\right) e^{\beta'\tau}\ d\tau\,\end{equation} then it suffices to show that when $K'\geq K'(\sigma)$ large enough for any $r'\in \RR$ there holds \begin{equation}\tilde\cI_{K'}(r'+2K') - 2\tilde\cI_{K'}(r'+K') + \tilde\cI_{K'}(r') > 0 \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-553: And by replacing $r'$ by $-r'-K'$ if necessary, we can assume without loss of generality that $\beta'\geq 2\sigma>0$.
elements.sent-2411-555: We can employ this formula to rewrite the quantity we wish to prove positive; elementary manipulations allow to write: \begin{equation}\;\;\;\;\; \tilde\cI_{K'}(r'+2K') - 2\tilde\cI_{K'}(r'+K') + \tilde\cI_{K'}(r') = e^{\beta'r'}\cdot\frac{(e^{\beta'K'}-1)^3}{\beta'} + \Re\left( e^{\beta''r'}\cdot\frac{(e^{\beta''K'}-1)^3}{\beta''} \right) = \frac{e^{\beta'r'}(e^{\beta'K'}-1)^3}{|\beta''|} \left[ \frac{|\beta''|}{\beta'} + \Re\left( \frac{|\beta''|e^{i\alpha'(r'+3K')}}{\beta''}\cdot \left(\frac{e^{\beta'K'}-e^{-i\alpha'K'}}{e^{\beta'K'} - 1}\right)^3 \right) \right] \geq \frac{e^{\beta'r'}(e^{\beta'K'}-1)^3}{|\beta''|} \left[ \frac{|\beta''|}{\beta'} - \left(\frac{|e^{\beta'K'}-e^{-i\alpha'K'}|}{e^{\beta'K'} - 1}\right)^3 \right]\,.\end{equation}. Since \begin{equation}\left(\frac{|e^{\beta'K'}-e^{-i\alpha'K'}|}{e^{\beta'K'} - 1}\right)^2 = \frac{\left(e^{\beta'K'}-\cos(\alpha'K')\right)^2 + \sin^2(\alpha'K')}{(e^{\beta'K'} - 1)^2} \leq \left( 1 + \frac{\min\{\alpha'K', 1\}^2}{e^{\beta'K'}-1}\right)^2 + \frac{\min\{\alpha'K', 1\}^2}{(e^{\beta'K'} - 1)^2} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-556: Thus when $\beta'K'$ is large enough (arranged by requiring $K\geq K_0(\sigma)$ for suitable $K_0(\sigma)$), we have in fact \begin{equation}\left(\frac{|e^{\beta'K'}-e^{-i\alpha'K'}|}{e^{\beta'K'} - 1}\right)^6 \leq 1 + \frac{20\min\{\alpha'K', 1\}^2}{e^{\beta'K'}-1} < 1 + \frac{(\alpha'K')^2}{(\beta'K')^2} = \left(\frac{|\beta''|}{\beta'} \right)^2,\end{equation} which implies the claim.

elements.sent-2411-768: We obtain a generic regularity result for stationary integral $n$-varifolds with only strongly isolated singularities inside $N$-dimensional Riemannian manifolds, in absence of any restriction on the dimension ($n\geq 2$) and codimension.
elements.sent-2411-769: In other words, only ``more complicated'' singularities may possibly persist.
elements.sent-2411-770: This implies, for instance, a generic finiteness result for the class of all closed minimal hypersurfaces of area at most $4\pi^2-\varepsilon$ (for any $\varepsilon>0$) in nearly round four-spheres: we can thus give precise answers, in the negative, to the questions of persistence of the Clifford football and of Hsiang's hyperspheres in nearly round metrics.
elements.sent-2411-771: The aforementioned main regularity result is achieved as a consequence of the fine analysis of the Fredholm index of the Jacobi operator for such varifolds: we prove on the one hand an exact formula relating that number to the Morse indices of the conical links at the singular points, while on the other hand we show that the same number is non-negative for all such varifolds if the ambient metric is generic.
elements.sent-2411-772: In 1983, Hsiang \cite{Hsi83 disproved Chern's spherical Bernstein conjecture by constructing a sequence of infinitely many, pairwise distinct, embedded minimal hyperspheres in the round four-dimensional sphere.}
elements.sent-2411-773: Such hyperspheres are constructed by means of an equivariant reduction and the analysis of the resulting singular ODE system in the quotient space.
elements.sent-2411-774: It is an interesting question, brought to the attention of the first-named author about a decade ago by Neves, whether such a picture persists when considering nearly round metrics on $S^4$.
elements.sent-2411-775: In particular, one may ask whether for any metric $g$ sufficiently close to the round one --- say in the smooth topology --- the Riemannian manifold $(S^4,g)$ still contains infinitely many (embedded) minimal hyperspheres.
elements.sent-2411-776: This problem stems from the well-known link between rigidity phenomena characterizing special submanifolds in round spheres, and the resulting scarcity phenomena for slightly deformed metrics.
elements.sent-2411-777: Indeed, as a reflex of the well-known characterization of simple closed geodesics on the round two-sphere as equatorial circles it was proven by Morse that there are nearly round metrics on $S^2$ that have only three simple closed geodesics, and similarly (as a reflex of the aforementioned rigidity theorems by Almgren and Calabi) White \cite{Whi91 proved that there are nearly round metrics on $S^3$ that have only four minimal hyperspheres.}
elements.sent-2411-778: In principle, one may attack this problem by means of a perturbative approach of essentially PDE-theoretic nature, by first showing (ideally) that any nearly round metric allows for a --- suitably defined --- singular minimal subvariety modelled on the aforementioned Clifford football, and then desingularizing the football in question to obtain the desired minimal hyperspheres.
elements.sent-2411-779: In the statements above and throughout this article the notion of ``genericity'' is understood in the sense of Baire, i.\,e.
elements.sent-2411-780: A well-known result by White (see \cites{Whi91, Whi17 ) ensures that the set of smooth ($C^\infty$) Riemannian metrics such that all closed, (smooth) embedded minimal hypersurfaces are non-degenerate (namely: have no non-trivial Jacobi fields) is indeed generic.}
elements.sent-2411-781: We can thus assume to have fixed, once and for all, a subset $\mathscr{G}$ of the class of smooth Riemannian metrics enjoying such a property, and when we write --- for instance --- that $g\in\mathscr{N}$ is a generic metric we may convene that $\mathscr{N}\subset\mathscr{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-782: Now, the connection with the geometric question we started with, and more specifically with the problem of perturbing the Clifford football to nearby stationary varifolds lies in the fact that, if we impose an area bound --- that is to say: a suitable upper bound on the mass of our stationary integral varifolds --- then all such varifolds, if mod 2 cyclic (cf.
elements.sent-2411-783: Informally speaking, one can say that, under such a bound, the Clifford football represents, in terms of regularity, the worst that can possibly happen.
elements.sent-2411-784: We recall that $2\pi^2$ equals the area of an equatorial $S^3$ in round $S^4$; thus note that the conclusion of this corollary cannot possibly hold if we remove the genericity assumption (one counterexample being indeed the round metric).
elements.sent-2411-785: Furthermore, we expect the threshold $4\pi^2$ to be sharp, based on the following heuristic argument.
